title: Pakistan Formally Announces Resumption of Operation Ghazab-il-Haq
detail: Pakistan's Foreign Office issued an official statement on March 31 formally announcing the full resumption of Operation Ghazab-il-Haq, declaring the operation would continue 'until its objectives are achieved.' The statement described the campaign as a 'precise, targeted operation' against Taliban military leadership, command infrastructure, logistics networks, and facilitators of terrorist activities. Pakistan's military conducted strikes across Nangarhar, Paktia, Kunar, and Khost provinces throughout the day. The declaration came six days after the Eid ceasefire collapsed and marked a formal return to full-intensity operations after the pause. Pakistan's spokesperson said the military had been 'patient' during the ceasefire period but that Taliban forces continued to provide sanctuary to TTP militants — the core justification for the operation — making resumption necessary.

title: Torkham Border Crossing Partially Reopened for Afghan National Returns
detail: Pakistan partially reopened the Torkham border crossing on March 31 to allow Afghan nationals to return to Afghanistan, following its closure since the Afghan sniper incident on March 27. The reopening was limited to Afghan nationals returning home and did not include normal trade or passenger services, which remained suspended. Initial reports indicated hundreds of individuals crossed during the partial reopening, though precise figures varied across sources. The Torkham crossing had been a repeated flashpoint in the conflict, having been shut since February 26 and only intermittently open since. Pakistan's decision to allow returns was described as a humanitarian gesture, but Pakistani officials made clear the crossing would remain closed to commercial traffic until security conditions improved. Afghanistan's Taliban government demanded full reopening and criticized Pakistan's 'collective punishment' approach to border management.
